result.stdout:
Usage: spiderly [command] [options]

* init
  This command initializes a new Spiderly project with a .NET backend and an 
Angular frontend. You can run it using spiderly init, and we will dynamically 
ask you for all the required information.

  --name  App name without spaces (you can change it later).
  --db  Database provider: sqlserver or postgresql (you can change it later).
  --db-connection-string  Full EF Core connection string. Bypasses 
auto-discovery and Docker.
  --pm  Package manager: npm (default), pnpm, yarn, or bun.

  Examples:
    $ spiderly init
    $ spiderly init --name MyApp --db postgresql
    $ spiderly init --name MyApp --db postgresql --pm pnpm
    $ spiderly init --name MyApp --db postgresql --db-connection-string 
"Host=localhost;Port=5432;Database=myapp;Username=postgres;Password=secret"

* add-new-entity
  This command creates a new entity and generates all necessary files: Entity 
class, Angular pages (list/details), routes, and menu item.

  --name  Entity name in PascalCase (e.g., Product). Required in non-interactive
mode.
  --data-view  Generate DataView template instead of Table template.

  Examples:
    $ spiderly add-new-entity
    $ spiderly add-new-entity --name Product
    $ spiderly add-new-entity --name Product --data-view

* add-migration
  Create a new EF Core migration.

  <name>  The name for the new migration.

  Example:
    $ spiderly add-migration YourMigrationName

* update-database
  Apply pending migrations to the database.

  Example:
    $ spiderly update-database

* remove-migration
  Remove the last migration.

  Example:
    $ spiderly remove-migration

* list-migrations
  List all available migrations.

  Example:
    $ spiderly list-migrations

* init

"Host=localhost;Port=5432;Database=myapp;Username=postgres;Password=secret"
